0.text: We came for the Igalo mud treatments and left with memories of the whole bay. Having a car meant we could visit Kotor between spa sessions and catch the Savina Monastery gardens in the morning light. Perfectly organised rental.

1.text: The botanical gardens of Herceg Novi were magical in spring, and the car made it so easy to drive to Dubrovnik for the day. The Debeli Brijeg crossing took barely twenty minutes. All the documents were ready at pickup.

2.text: Forte Mare at sunset, the view from Savina across the bay, and then the long golden drive back along the water — I photographed it all. The car was clean, the handover was smooth, and they even suggested the best light for the fortress.

0.a: Bring a valid driving licence showing Latin-script details, your passport, and a physical credit card in the main driver's name. If your licence was issued outside the EU or EEA, an International Driving Permit is recommended. Electronic copies and photocopies are not accepted by rental agents.

1.q: Can I drive to Croatia or Albania?
1.a: Yes — and from Herceg Novi it is remarkably easy. Dubrovnik is only 30 km away via the Debeli Brijeg border crossing. Let us know at booking and we will ensure the Green Card and cross-border documentation are ready before you collect the car.

2.a: The minimum age to rent is 21, and drivers must hold a full licence for at least two years. Drivers aged 21–24 pay a young driver supplement, and the exact amount varies by vehicle class — it is shown clearly in the search results before you confirm.

3.a: All vehicles include Third Party Liability insurance and most include CDW in the base price. VAT, a mandatory equipment kit (first-aid box, warning triangle, reflective vest), and a transparent fuel policy are also included. Mileage terms vary by vehicle — check the listing details. Extended zero-excess cover is available as an add-on.

4.a: We offer delivery to Herceg Novi town centre, the Igalo spa quarter, or a meet-and-greet at Tivat Airport arrivals — 35 km along the bay road, roughly 40 minutes. Choose your preferred location during booking and the agent will confirm the exact meeting point.

5.a: Standard vehicles offer free cancellation up to 7 days before the rental start. Luxury cars and convertibles require 30 days' notice for a free cancellation. A 6% payment processing fee applies to all cancellations. If you need to reschedule around a spa appointment or day trip, contact us and we will do our best to accommodate.

6.a: A security deposit is held on a physical credit card — the amount ranges from €50 to €300 depending on the vehicle category. Some economy cars are available without a deposit. The hold is released within a few working days after you return the car in its original condition.

7.q: What is the Verige ferry and how much does it cost?
7.a: The Kamenari-Lepetane ferry crosses the Bay of Kotor mouth in 5 minutes, running every 15 minutes. It costs about 5 EUR per car (cash only) and saves 30-45 minutes compared to driving the full bay road to Tivat. From Herceg Novi, drive south to Kamenari (10 minutes) and queue for the next crossing.

aboutBody.h1: Herceg Novi Car Hire — Rooted in the City of Sun
aboutBody.p1: Herceg Novi is not just a starting point — it is a destination in its own right. Known locally as the City of Sun and the Botanical Garden of Montenegro, this fortress town at the entrance to Boka Bay has drawn wellness seekers and garden lovers for more than a century. We have been helping visitors navigate its lush riviera and the Dubrovnik corridor since 2007.
aboutBody.p2: The story of Herceg Novi is inseparable from Igalo, the spa quarter that stretches west along the bay shore. The Igalo Institute, founded in 1949, pioneered therapeutic mud treatments, mineral baths, and hydrotherapy programmes that still attract visitors from across Europe. Our car hire service grew from a simple observation: guests staying for spa treatments wanted the freedom to explore the coast between sessions — to drive to Dubrovnik for lunch, catch the Verige ferry to Tivat, or climb the stairways to Savina Monastery's rose garden.
aboutBody.p3: We provide rental cars with pickup and delivery across the Herceg Novi Riviera — from the Igalo spa quarter and the town centre to Tivat Airport, 35 km along the bay. Our fleet ranges from nimble city cars that handle the narrow staircase streets to spacious SUVs for the mountain roads inland. Every vehicle is fully insured with no hidden fees.
aboutBody.promise1: Full insurance with zero excess options available
aboutBody.promise2: Delivery to Igalo, Herceg Novi centre, or airport meet-and-greet at Tivat
aboutBody.promise3: No hidden fees — the price you see is the price you pay
aboutBody.promise4: Cross-border travel permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
aboutBody.promise6: 24/7 roadside assistance from the promenade to the border
aboutBody.p4: We know which stairway leads to Kanli Kula for the sunset, which season the mimosa trees blanket the hillside in gold, and exactly when the Debeli Brijeg border queue thins out for a smooth Dubrovnik crossing. We can point you to the boat taxi for Žanjic beach, the hidden path to Forte Mare overlooking the harbour, and the best seafood along the Šetalište promenade. Whether your itinerary centres on Igalo's therapeutic mud, a Savina Monastery morning, or a full Boka Bay road trip, we will help you plan every turn.
aboutBody.p5: Questions about renting a car in Herceg Novi? Curious about the Verige ferry shortcut or the best time to cross into Croatia? We are available via phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Search our fleet online and book in minutes — or reach out and let us help you map the perfect riviera drive.

kotorBody.h1: Kotor — From Mimosa Groves to Medieval Walls
kotorBody.p1: The 45-minute drive from Herceg Novi to Kotor is one of Europe's great coastal journeys. The road hugs the waterline of Boka Bay, passing through Risan's ancient mosaic quarter and the baroque stillness of Perast before the medieval walls of Kotor appear around the final bend. After mornings spent among the botanical gardens and spa terraces of Herceg Novi, Kotor provides a striking counterpoint — all stone ramparts, candlelit churches, and narrow lanes climbing toward St. John's Fortress.
kotorBody.p2: A rental car from Herceg Novi gives you the freedom to linger in Perast on the way, stop at a waterside restaurant in Risan, and arrive in Kotor on your own schedule. Parking can be scarce in summer near the Old Town gates, but larger car parks on the approach roads handle the overflow well.
kotorBody.stJohnText: The climb to St. John's Fortress follows 1,350 steps up the mountainside behind the Old Town. The panoramic view from the top — the entire bay spread below, cruise ships reduced to white specks, the Adriatic shimmering beyond the mouth — rewards every step. The Contarini Tower and Our Lady of Health Church mark the ascent.
kotorBody.cathedralText: Built in 809 AD and reconstructed after the 1124 earthquake, St. Tryphon Cathedral anchors Kotor's main square. Its twin bell towers and Romanesque facade house Byzantine frescoes, gold and silver reliquaries, and a quiet interior that contrasts sharply with the busy piazza outside.
kotorBody.perastTrip: Perast sits 20 minutes back along the bay road toward Herceg Novi. The baroque village faces two tiny islands — St. George, with its cypress-shaded Benedictine monastery, and Our Lady of the Rocks, a man-made island where sailors have tossed rocks since 1452. A water taxi from the harbour costs a few euros.
kotorBody.cetinjeTrip: The old royal capital of Cetinje lies an hour inland through dramatic mountain switchbacks. The monastery houses sacred relics, while the former Royal Court preserves the artefacts and photographs of Montenegro's brief kingdom. The drive itself, climbing from sea level to the Lovćen plateau, is as memorable as the destination.
kotorBody.royalCourtText: Montenegro's royal palace from 1871, now a museum displaying art, artefacts, and photographs from the nation's royal history. Nestled beside the monastery in the quiet streets of the former capital.

budvaBody.h1: Budva — Sandy Riviera Beyond the Spa Gardens
budvaBody.p1: An hour south of Herceg Novi's botanical terraces, the coastline transforms. The sheltered bay gives way to open Adriatic, and Budva appears — Montenegro's liveliest beach town, where 2,500 years of history meet a thoroughly modern summer scene. The contrast with Herceg Novi's meditative spa atmosphere is part of the appeal: morning mud treatment in Igalo, afternoon on Jaz Beach, evening in a Budva Old Town restaurant.
budvaBody.p2: The drive from Herceg Novi takes you along the bay road to Kotor, through the tunnel at Trojica, and down the southern coast to Budva. With a rental car you can explore the entire riviera at your own rhythm, stopping at Sveti Stefan for photographs and Bečići for swimming.
budvaBody.beachesIntro: The Budva Riviera stretches for 21 kilometres, offering sand and pebble beaches along the open Adriatic:
budvaBody.mogren: Two connected coves reached through a cliff tunnel, minutes from the Old Town walls — intimate and sheltered
budvaBody.jaz: A wide sandy bay popular with locals and summer festival crowds, backed by green hillsides
budvaBody.svetiStefan: The iconic fortified islet visible from the coast road, with a public beach on the mainland side
budvaBody.becici: A long stretch of fine sand once voted among Europe's most beautiful beaches — quieter than Budva proper
budvaBody.oldTownText: Budva's Old Town is a compact walled peninsula of cobblestone streets, Venetian architecture, seventh-century churches, and a lively mix of restaurants and galleries. The Citadela fortress at the southern tip frames panoramic views of the coast and the island of Sveti Nikola. After the tranquil gardens of Herceg Novi, the energy of Budva's piazzas feels like stepping into a different country.
budvaBody.svetiStefanTrip: Just 6 km south, this photogenic fortified island village is now a luxury hotel, but the views from the road above are spectacular and free. The mainland beach is open to all visitors.
budvaBody.kotorTrip: A 30-minute drive north through the Trojica tunnel brings you to Kotor and its UNESCO-listed medieval quarter — or continue along the bay road back toward Herceg Novi for the full circuit.
budvaBody.lovcenTrip: Head inland for the Lovćen mountain plateau and the Njegoš Mausoleum at 1,657 metres. On a clear day, the views stretch across four countries. The winding ascent from the coast is an experience in itself.
budvaBody.gettingAroundText: While Budva's Old Town rewards walking, a rental car is essential for reaching the riviera beaches, coastal villages, and inland viewpoints. Parking is available near the Old Town walls and at several public lots along the main road. In peak summer, arriving early in the morning guarantees the best spots.

perastBody.h1: Perast — Baroque Calm on the Bay
perastBody.p1: Perast sits half an hour along the bay from Herceg Novi, where the waterway narrows between steep mountain walls. After the lush gardens and spa terraces of the City of Sun, Perast offers something different — a village of baroque stone palazzos, silent bell towers, and still water reflecting the two tiny islands offshore. The pace here slows to the rhythm of small fishing boats crossing to Our Lady of the Rocks.
perastBody.p2: Under Venetian rule from 1420 to 1797, Perast earned fame for its master shipbuilders and fearless sailors. That maritime heritage is carved into every palazzo facade and preserved in the town museum. With fewer than 850 residents, the village feels like a private gallery — intimate, unhurried, and achingly photogenic.
perastBody.stGeorgeText: A natural island just offshore, shaded by a dark grove of cypresses and home to a Benedictine monastery dating from the 12th century. The island is visible from every waterfront cafe in Perast, and small boats from the harbour will carry visitors across for a few euros.
perastBody.ladyOfRocksText: This man-made island was created by generations of sailors tossing rocks and sinking old boats into the bay. Every July 22nd, locals fill their vessels with stones and continue the tradition. The church holds thousands of silver votive tablets and a collection of Baroque paintings. A water taxi from the Perast harbour takes five minutes.
perastBody.historicText: Perast's waterfront is lined with Renaissance and Baroque palaces — remnants of the merchant families who competed for the grandest facade. The Museum of Perast, housed in the Palata Bujović (widely considered the most beautiful building on the Montenegrin coast), contains archives, naval paintings, and town records spanning centuries of Venetian and Adriatic commerce.
perastBody.monasteryText: A few kilometres north along the bay road toward Risan, the Banja Monastery shelters ancient religious art from Russian, Greek, and Boka traditions. The detour is brief and the setting — nestled into the hillside above the water — is quietly beautiful.

tivatBody.h1: Tivat — Marina Glamour Across the Narrows
tivatBody.p1: Tivat lies directly across the Verige Strait from Herceg Novi — a 10-minute ferry crossing that feels like stepping between two different worlds. Where Herceg Novi wraps itself in mimosa trees and Ottoman stairways, Tivat gleams with polished marina decks and waterfront cocktail bars. The town hosts one of Montenegro's two commercial airports, making it an incredibly convenient gateway for visitors arriving by air.
tivatBody.p2: With around 13,000 residents, Tivat blends small-town Mediterranean ease with the cosmopolitan energy brought by Porto Montenegro. Visitors stroll the seafront boardwalk, browse boutiques in narrow alleyways, and dine at waterside restaurants where fresh Adriatic seafood arrives daily.
tivatBody.beachesText: Tivat claims 17 beaches along 3 kilometres of coast, recognised among the finest on the inner bay:
tivatBody.beach1: Plavi Horizanti (Blue Horizons): White sand and crystal-clear water, ideal for swimming and afternoon sunbathing
tivatBody.beach2: Przno on Sveti Marko Island: A secluded former resort with pristine water, perfect for paddleboarding and swimming
tivatBody.portoText: The centrepiece of modern Tivat is Porto Montenegro — a luxury superyacht marina and waterfront village that has repositioned the town as one of the Adriatic's most fashionable addresses. The complex houses designer boutiques, fine restaurants, a pool club, and a Naval Heritage Collection museum tracing Boka Bay's centuries of seafaring tradition.
tivatBody.monasteryText: Tenth-century ruins on the Prevlaka peninsula at the mouth of Boka Bay, with ongoing archaeological work uncovering layers of medieval monastic life. The setting, at the very tip of the peninsula with water on three sides, is serene.
tivatBody.churchText: Built in 1734, this parish church contains religious paintings, gilded woodwork, and offers views across the bay toward the mountains behind Herceg Novi.
tivatBody.museumText: Kotor's Maritime Museum, just 10 minutes along the bay road, displays Montenegro's seafaring heritage — model ships, navigational instruments, and portraits of the admirals who once commanded Boka's merchant fleets.

tivatAirportBody.p1: Tivat Airport sits on the inner shore of Boka Bay, just 4 km from Tivat town centre and 35 km from Herceg Novi. For visitors heading to the City of Sun and its spa quarter, this is the closest airport — and the most scenic arrival, with the bay unfolding around every turn of the approach road.
tivatAirportBody.p2: Year-round daily flights connect to Belgrade, with charter and seasonal services expanding heavily during the summer months. The airport is renowned among pilots for its challenging approach through the surrounding mountains — and among travellers for the stunning views on descent over the bay.
tivatAirportBody.dist1: Herceg Novi: approximately 35 minutes (or 20 min via Verige ferry)
tivatAirportBody.facilitiesText: The terminal offers cafes, duty-free shopping, souvenir stores, and taxi ranks outside arrivals. Bus services run to Herceg Novi and Budva, but a rental car gives you the freedom to reach the Igalo spa quarter, the botanical gardens, or the Dubrovnik border on your own schedule.
tivatAirportBody.whyText: Tivat Airport puts you within minutes of Montenegro's most celebrated coastal destinations. Head north along the bay toward Kotor and Perast, south through the tunnel to Budva's beaches, or west across the Verige ferry to Herceg Novi's garden-draped riviera. No transfers, no bus timetables — just straight from the terminal to the open road.
tivatAirportBody.meetText: We meet you at the arrivals hall with your name on a sign. Your car is parked right outside. From landing to driving: about 10 minutes.

podgoricaBody.h1: Podgorica — The Capital on Six Rivers
podgoricaBody.p1: Most international flights into Montenegro land at Podgorica Airport (TGD), 9 km from the city centre. As the capital and largest city, Podgorica is home to around 170,000 residents and sits at the confluence of six rivers — the Morača, Ribnica, Zeta, Sitnica, Maréza, and Cijevna. The drive west to Herceg Novi takes approximately two hours through dramatic mountain scenery.
podgoricaBody.p2: The city layers Ottoman footprint over medieval stone, communist-era functionalist blocks alongside contemporary glass towers. The Millennium Bridge has become a symbol of Podgorica's modern identity — a sweeping white pylon over the Morača that photographs beautifully at dusk.
podgoricaBody.p3: Podgorica is Montenegro's cultural heart. Visitors who rush straight to the coast without pausing here miss the galleries, the riverside parks, and the warmth of a capital city that moves at its own unhurried pace.
podgoricaBody.seeText: Browse Italian fabric shops and local wine merchants in the Stara Varoš quarter. Visit Sat-Kula, the Ottoman clock tower, and the archaeological ruins at Duklja — a Roman city dating back two millennia. The Cathedral of the Resurrection of Christ, completed in 2013, is one of the largest Orthodox churches in the Balkans.
podgoricaBody.skadarText: One of the most beautiful lakes in southern Europe, straddling the Montenegro-Albania border as a National Park. Home to vast colonies of Dalmatian pelicans, the lake is the largest in the Balkans and reachable in 30 minutes from the capital. Boat tours weave through water lily channels past ancient island monasteries.
podgoricaBody.danilovgradText: A quiet river town 30 minutes west of Podgorica, offering riverside swimming spots, galleries, and the nearby Zdrebaonik monastery. The Zeta valley setting is peaceful — a welcome contrast to the capital's bustle.
podgoricaBody.zabljak: An alpine town in the Durmitor mountains at 1,500 metres, about 170 km north of Podgorica. Year-round appeal: skiing in winter, hiking the Tara Canyon rim, white-water rafting, and mountain biking through pine forests in summer.

podgoricaAirportBody.p1: Podgorica Airport serves as Montenegro's principal gateway, handling the majority of international and budget-carrier flights. Each year more routes open as the country's tourism profile grows — though many visitors still combine a Podgorica arrival with a coastal transfer to the Herceg Novi Riviera.
podgoricaAirportBody.p2: The current terminal opened in 2006. The old terminal building now functions as an aviation history museum — a charming footnote for plane enthusiasts. The airport code TGD is a relic of the city's former name, Titograd.
podgoricaAirportBody.distIntro: The airport lies 12 km south of Podgorica city centre.
podgoricaAirportBody.flightsText: Daily service to Belgrade year-round, with regular connections to Paris, Rome, Frankfurt, Vienna, Budapest, and Ljubljana. Seasonal routes expand the network considerably in summer. Budget carriers increasingly serve TGD, making Podgorica an affordable entry point for a Montenegrin road trip.
podgoricaAirportBody.facilitiesText: The terminal offers eight departure gates, two arrival gates, cafes, duty-free shopping, and over 25,000 square metres of parking. Despite its modest size, the airport has won the ACI Europe award for the best airport under one million passengers.
podgoricaAirportBody.awardText: For travellers heading to the coast, the 2-hour drive to Herceg Novi crosses the Montenegrin interior — through the Morača river canyon, past mountain villages, and down to the bay. It is one of the most scenic airport-to-hotel transfers in Europe.
podgoricaAirportBody.rentalText: We meet you at the arrivals hall and bring the car to you. No queues, no off-site shuttle lots. Within minutes of clearing customs, you will be on the road — heading west toward the botanical coast or south to Budva's sandy riviera.

dubrovnikAirportBody.h1: Fly into Dubrovnik, Drive to the City of Sun
dubrovnikAirportBody.p1: Herceg Novi is the closest Montenegrin town to Dubrovnik Airport — just 30 km and a single border crossing at Debeli Brijeg. For visitors wanting to combine Croatia's most famous walled city with Montenegro's botanical riviera, flying into Dubrovnik and driving south is the most efficient route. The airport receives flights from across Europe, many of which do not yet serve Montenegro directly.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p2: The border crossing is straightforward provided your rental paperwork and Green Card insurance are prepared in advance. We arrange all cross-border documentation before you arrive, so the handover at the airport is seamless.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p3: From the moment you clear the Debeli Brijeg checkpoint, the landscape shifts. The limestone karst gives way to the lush, garden-draped hillsides of Herceg Novi — mimosa trees, citrus groves, and the warm microclimate that earned this town its City of Sun title. The drive from airport to Igalo spa quarter takes roughly 40 minutes.
dubrovnikAirportBody.borderText: During peak summer months, the Debeli Brijeg crossing can back up for an hour or more. Crossing early in the morning or after 8pm avoids the worst queues. Ensure your rental company has provided a Green Card covering Montenegro and Croatia — without it, border guards will not let you through.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText1: The airport terminal sits on the E65 highway, less than 30 minutes from Dubrovnik's Old Town. Parking is limited and fills quickly in high season — pre-booking is advisable if you plan to leave a car here. Payment machines accept cash and cards.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText2: Inside the terminal: bureau de change, banking services, a post office, cafes, duty-free shopping, and a newsstand. The layout is compact and easy to navigate.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingTitle: Getting to the Herceg Novi Riviera
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText1: From Dubrovnik Airport, follow the E65 south toward Montenegro. The Debeli Brijeg border crossing is about 20 km from the terminal. After clearing both the Croatian exit and Montenegrin entry checkpoints, the road descends into Herceg Novi within 15 minutes.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText2: The arrival is memorable. The road drops through pine forest and suddenly the Bay of Kotor opens before you — Herceg Novi's fortress towers, the Igalo waterfront, and the botanical hillsides tumbling down to the Adriatic. It is one of the most dramatic first impressions of any Mediterranean town.

montenegroBody.h1: Exploring Montenegro by Car from the City of Sun
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro is one of Europe's most rewarding self-drive destinations — and Herceg Novi, perched at the entrance to Boka Bay, is the ideal starting point. From the botanical gardens and spa terraces of the City of Sun, every major destination in the country is reachable within a few hours: medieval Kotor along the bay, the sandy riviera of Budva to the south, Durmitor's alpine peaks to the north, and Dubrovnik just 30 km across the Croatian border.
montenegroBody.p2: Despite its compact size, Montenegro demands patience on the road. Mountain switchbacks, narrow coastal lanes, and panoramic distractions at every turn mean actual driving times often exceed what maps suggest. This is not a complaint — the journey itself is the experience, whether you are hugging the bay waterline past Perast or climbing the serpentines to Lovćen.
montenegroBody.startText: Most visitors collect their rental car at Tivat Airport (35 min from Herceg Novi via the bay road, or 20 min via the Verige ferry shortcut), at Podgorica Airport (2 hours inland), or at Dubrovnik Airport (30 km north across the Croatian border). We also deliver directly to hotels and the Igalo spa quarter in Herceg Novi.
montenegroBody.timesText1: The bay road to Kotor takes about 45 minutes, winding along the waterline through Risan and Perast. Budva is roughly an hour south via Kotor and the Trojica tunnel. Podgorica sits 2 hours inland through the Morača canyon. The Dubrovnik border at Debeli Brijeg is just 15 minutes north.
montenegroBody.timesText2: The Verige ferry, crossing the bay narrows between Kamenari and Lepetane, saves approximately 45 minutes compared to driving around the entire inner bay. It runs frequently and accepts cars — a local shortcut worth knowing.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Traffic police checks are common but present no concern with proper documentation. You will need:
montenegroBody.rule1: A valid driving licence (international licence accepted)
montenegroBody.rule2: Rental contract or proof of vehicle ownership
montenegroBody.rule4: A Green Card for border crossings (approximately €15 for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts are mandatory for all passengers
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Mobile phone use while driving is prohibited
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Any drinking and driving is illegal — zero tolerance
montenegroBody.conditionsText: Town and bay-road surfaces are generally good. Mountain roads can be narrower and more challenging, with minimal guard rails in places. Some local drivers take corners confidently — exercise caution on mountain passes and the tighter stretches of the bay road, especially between Herceg Novi and Risan where the lane narrows.
montenegroBody.e65Text: The primary coastal artery connects Herceg Novi to Kotor, continuing past Tivat to Budva and Podgorica. This route hugs the entire inner bay and offers some of the most celebrated views in the Mediterranean — mountain walls dropping sheer into deep blue water, with Perast's bell towers and island churches reflected on the surface.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Bisects the country from coast to interior, climbing through the Montenegrin highlands toward eastern Bosnia. This route passes through dramatically different terrain — from maritime gardens to highland pastures within an hour.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border travel is straightforward with the right documentation. The closest crossing from Herceg Novi is Debeli Brijeg (Croatia) — just 15 minutes north. Peak-season queues can stretch for a kilometre at popular crossings, so plan for weekday mornings or late evenings when possible.
montenegroBody.nationTitle: A Young Nation with Ancient Roots
montenegroBody.nationText: Montenegro declared independence in June 2006, making it one of the world's youngest nations. Yet its cultural heritage spans millennia — from Roman mosaics in Risan to Venetian fortresses in Kotor, from Ottoman clock towers in Herceg Novi to Austro-Hungarian villas in Igalo. For the self-drive traveller, this layered history unfolds around every bay bend and mountain pass.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Can I Take My Rental Car Across Borders?
borderCrossingBody.p1: Yes. Cross-border travel is permitted from Montenegro to Croatia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. Notify your rental company at booking so they can prepare the necessary documentation — including the all-important Green Card insurance.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card is an international motor insurance certificate proving your vehicle is insured in the country you are entering. It is required at every Balkan border crossing.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Where to get it: your rental company arranges this — confirm at booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Countries covered: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Kosovo, Serbia
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Without a Green Card, border guards will not let you through. Always verify the card covers all countries on your planned route before setting off.
borderCrossingBody.docsTitle: Documents You Need at the Border
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Valid passport or EU ID card
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration document (provided with the car)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The most popular crossing for Herceg Novi visitors. Debeli Brijeg sits on the E65 coastal road just 15 minutes north of town — the quickest route to Dubrovnik, 30 km away. This crossing gets extremely busy in summer: expect queues of 1-2 hours during peak July and August weekends.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Best times to cross: early morning (before 8am) or late evening (after 8pm). Weekday mornings are significantly quieter than weekends. We recommend leaving the Igalo spa quarter by 7am for a stress-free crossing.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: The Kamenari-Lepetane ferry (Verige ferry) shortcuts across the bay narrows, saving approximately 45 minutes compared to driving the full inner bay circuit to reach crossings further south.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: Two main crossings: Sukobin/Muriqan (coastal, near Ulcinj) and Hani i Hotit (inland, near Skadar Lake). The coastal crossing is more tourist-friendly and typically has shorter wait times. Both are approximately 3-4 hours south of Herceg Novi.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Note: Albanian road surfaces and driving culture differ markedly from Montenegro. Drive cautiously, especially on rural roads.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: The main crossing at Šćepan Polje (near Foča) connects to the Tara river canyon area. Via Vilusi from Nikšić offers an alternative. Crossings are generally quiet with minimal wait times outside peak summer.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: Cross at Dobrakovo on the E65 north of Bijelo Polje. This crossing connects directly to the Serbian motorway network toward Belgrade.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The crossing at Kula (near Rožaje) connects to Peja/Peć. Check with your rental company that Kosovo is specifically included on your Green Card — some policies exclude it.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Have all documents ready before you reach the checkpoint — do not search for them at the window
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Turn off your engine while queuing (border guards notice and appreciate it)
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Summer weekends are worst — cross on weekdays wherever possible
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Debeli Brijeg (closest to Herceg Novi) is the busiest; inland crossings are quieter
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Keep cash in Euros — useful for tolls and purchases on the Croatian side
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check your mobile data plan covers the country you are entering — roaming charges apply
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Driving Rules That Change at Borders
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Each neighbouring country has slightly different traffic laws. Key differences:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: headlights must be on at all times. Motorway tolls apply (cash or card)
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on rural roads. Road quality varies significantly
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres mandatory November-April. Carry first-aid kit and warning triangle
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: zero alcohol tolerance for drivers. Motorway vignette required
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: drive on the right. International driving permit recommended
borderCrossingBody.popularTitle: Popular Cross-Border Routes from Herceg Novi
borderCrossingBody.route1: Herceg Novi → Dubrovnik: 30 min via Debeli Brijeg. The classic day trip from the Riviera
borderCrossingBody.route2: Herceg Novi → Mostar (Bosnia): 3-4 hrs via Trebinje. Visit the famous Stari Most bridge
borderCrossingBody.route3: Budva → Shkodër (Albania): 2.5 hrs via the Ulcinj coastal crossing
borderCrossingBody.route4: Podgorica → Belgrade (Serbia): 5-6 hrs via the E65 north
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Need a car with cross-border documentation ready? Search our fleet — select your destination countries at booking and we will arrange the Green Card and all necessary paperwork.
